note_data.title: Chemical Bonding
keyPoints: **Ionic bonding** occurs between metals and non-metals through transfer of electrons from metal atoms to non-metal atoms
**Covalent bonding** involves sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms
**Dative covalent bonding** forms when one atom provides both electrons in a shared pair
**Metallic bonding** involves electrostatic attraction between positive metal ions and delocalised electrons
**Electronegativity** is the ability of an atom to attract bonding electrons in a covalent bond
**VSEPR theory** predicts molecular shapes based on electron pair repulsion around central atom
**Van der Waals forces** are weak intermolecular forces including London dispersion forces and permanent dipole interactions
**Hydrogen bonding** occurs between hydrogen bonded to N, O, or F and lone pairs on N, O, or F atoms
**Bond polarity** depends on electronegativity difference between bonded atoms

definitions.definition: The electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer
The electrostatic attraction between two nuclei and a shared pair of electrons
A covalent bond in which one atom provides both electrons in the shared pair
The ability of an atom to attract bonding electrons in a covalent bond
Weak intermolecular forces of attraction between molecules

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Use VSEPR theory to determine shape based on electron pairs
Give similarities and differences with clear justification

modelAnswer.question: Explain why ammonia (NH₃) has a pyramidal shape and predict the bond angle. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Nitrogen has 4 electron pairs around it (3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair). The 4 electron pairs arrange themselves tetrahedrally to minimise repulsion. However, the molecular shape is pyramidal because only the bonding pairs determine the shape. The bond angle is less than 109.5° (approximately 107°) because lone pair-bonding pair repulsion is greater than bonding pair-bonding pair repulsion.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Pyramidal shape due to lone pair
Mark 3: Bond angle less than 109.5° due to lone pair repulsion

commonMistakes: Never say 'electrons are shared equally' in polar covalent bonds — always specify 'unequally shared'
Students often confuse molecular shape with electron pair geometry — shape depends only on bonding pairs
Never say 'molecules have ionic bonding' — ionic bonding exists between ions, not molecules
Students often forget that dative bonds are shown with arrows (→) not lines in diagrams
Never confuse intermolecular forces with intramolecular bonds — they are different types of attractions
examinerTips: Bond angles appear frequently in Paper 2 — always relate to electron pair repulsion
Draw dot-and-cross diagrams clearly showing outer electrons only for full marks
VSEPR questions worth 3-4 marks appear almost every year
Always state the number of electron pairs before predicting molecular shape
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: VSEPR shapes diagram showing linear (2 pairs), trigonal planar (3 pairs), tetrahedral (4 pairs), trigonal bipyramidal (5 pairs), and octahedral (6 pairs) arrangements with bond angles labeled
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the difference in melting points between sodium chloride and methane using bonding theory
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking which compound has the highest boiling point based on intermolecular forces
M/J 2022 P2 Q3c (4 marks) — students had to draw dot-and-cross diagrams for ammonia and ammonium ion, then explain the difference in bond formation
N/D 2023 P2 Q5a (3 marks) — required explanation of why graphite conducts electricity but diamond doesn't, relating to structure and bonding

workedExample.question: Explain why the boiling point of water (100°C) is much higher than that of hydrogen sulfide (-60°C), even though both are covalent compounds with similar molecular masses. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the intermolecular forces in each compound
Step 2: Water molecules form hydrogen bonds due to highly electronegative oxygen bonded to hydrogen
Step 3: Hydrogen sulfide only has weak van der Waals forces as sulfur is not electronegative enough for hydrogen bonding
Step 4: Relate strength of intermolecular forces to energy required to separate molecules
workedExample.answer: Water has hydrogen bonding between molecules because oxygen is highly electronegative and bonded to hydrogen. Hydrogen sulfide only has weak van der Waals forces because sulfur is not electronegative enough to form hydrogen bonds. Hydrogen bonds are much stronger than van der Waals forces, so more energy is needed to separate water molecules, resulting in a higher boiling point.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying hydrogen bonding in water, 1 mark for identifying weaker forces in H₂S, 1 mark for comparing strength of forces, 1 mark for linking to boiling point

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused intermolecular and intramolecular forces' — always specify whether you're discussing forces between molecules or within molecules when explaining physical properties

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often drew incorrect dot-and-cross diagrams with wrong number of electrons' — always count total valence electrons first, then check each atom has correct number of bonds/lone pairs

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently stated that ionic compounds conduct electricity without mentioning mobility of ions' — always explain that ions must be free to move (molten/dissolved) for electrical conduction
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must master drawing dot-and-cross diagrams showing: outer shell electrons only, different symbols for electrons from different atoms (dots vs crosses), correct bonding arrangements, and any lone pairs. For ionic compounds, show electron transfer with arrows and write charges on resulting ions. For covalent compounds, show shared electron pairs between atoms. Practice drawing water (bent), ammonia (pyramidal), methane (tetrahedral), and carbon dioxide (linear) structures.
note_data.quickRecap: Chemical bonds form when atoms achieve stable electron configurations. Ionic bonding involves electron transfer between metals and non-metals, creating oppositely charged ions. Covalent bonding involves electron sharing between non-metals. Metallic bonding involves delocalized electrons in a 'sea' around metal cations. Bond type determines physical properties like melting point, electrical conductivity, and solubility.
